query: What are the requirements and fees required to submit a valid interconnection application for a large generator in ISO New England, based on the latest ISO-NE Interconnection Process Guide and ISO-NE Tariff Schedule 22 Large Generator Interconnection Procedures (LGIP)?
final_answer:
# Requirements and Fees for Submitting a Valid Large Generator Interconnection Application in ISO New England

Based on the latest ISO-NE Interconnection Process Guide, Schedule 22 LGIP (effective 5/3/25, ER25-2149-000), and supporting Order No. 2023 compliance documents, below is a comprehensive summary of the requirements and fees.

---

## 1. Prerequisites Before Submission

| Requirement | Details | Source |
|---|---|---|
| **ISO Customer ID** | Must obtain an ISO Customer ID before or simultaneously with submission | [Interconnection Process Guide](https://www.iso-ne.com/participate/applications-status-changes/interconnection-process-guide) |
| **IR CEII Clearance** | Must obtain Interconnection Request Critical Energy Infrastructure Information access (can take up to 2 weeks) | [Tips for submitting TCS Agreement](https://askiso.iso-ne.com/s/article/Tips-for-submitting-a-Transitional-Cluster-Study-Agreement-package) |
| **IRTT Account** | Must create an account in the Interconnection Request Tracking Tool (IRTT) and be assigned the appropriate role | [Interconnection Process Guide](https://www.iso-ne.com/participate/applications-status-changes/interconnection-process-guide) |

---

## 2. Submission Window

Interconnection Requests can **only** be submitted during a **45 calendar day Cluster Request Window**, as required by FERC Order No. 2023.

- **Next Cluster Request Window**: Expected **October 5, 2026 to November 19, 2026**
- ISO-NE will have **5 Business Days** to review the data after receipt
- Interconnection Customer will have up to **10 Business Days** to cure any data deficiencies, but no later than the close of the Cluster Request Window

**Source**: [Interconnection Process Guide](https://www.iso-ne.com/participate/applications-status-changes/interconnection-process-guide) and [ISO-NE January 2024 TC Presentation (LGIP Section 3.4.4)](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100007/a03.1b_2024_01_23_tc_order2023_compliance_presentation.pdf)

---

## 3. Required Fees and Deposits

Per LGIP Section 3.4.1.1 and the Order No. 2023 compliance filings, the following financial commitments are required at the time of Interconnection Request submission:

| Fee/Deposit | Amount | Refundability | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Initial Deposit** | **$50,000** | Potentially non-refundable | Applied toward ISO costs for processing the IR, developing study agreements and attachments, and developing the LGIA |
| **Study Deposit (Cluster Study)** | **$250,000** (new NR or CNR requests) or **$100,000** (requests to change from existing NR to CNR) | Refundable (applied to Cluster Study costs) | Covers the Interconnection Customer's share of Cluster Study costs |
| **Commercial Readiness Deposit (CRD)** | Per Order No. 2023 requirements (ISO-NE adopted the federal standard) | Refundable minus applicable withdrawal penalties | Demonstrates commercial readiness; can be provided as cash, irrevocable Letter of Credit, Surety Bond, or combination |
| **Site Control Deposit** (alternative for NR IS only) | **$10,000/MW**, subject to a minimum of **$500,000** and maximum of **$2,000,000** | Refundable within the cure period (per Section 3.4.3) | Only available when Regulatory Limitations prevent demonstration of 100% Site Control for NR Interconnection Service |

**Source**: [LGIP Section 3.4.1.1 via Jan 2024 TC Presentation](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100007/a03.1b_2024_01_23_tc_order2023_compliance_presentation.pdf), [ISO-NE Transition Q&A (Q16, Q17)](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100006/2023_12_15_order_no_2023_transition_question_and_answer.pdf), [Appendix 1 IR Form](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100030/oatt-schedule-22-appendix-1-large-generator-lgip-interconnection-request-with-attachments.pdf)

### Additional Downstream Costs (Post-Submission)

| Item | Details |
|---|---|
| **Cluster Restudy Deposit** | Additional deposit for 5% of network upgrade cost assignment from the Cluster Study |
| **Interconnection Facilities Study Deposit** | Determined by the Interconnecting Transmission Owner and ISO |
| **Affected System Study Costs** | Interconnection Customer responsible for costs of studies of Internal Affected Systems (LGIP Section 3.4.4.1) |

---

## 4. Valid Interconnection Request Requirements

Per LGIP Section 3.4 and Appendix 1, a valid Interconnection Request must include all of the following:

### 4.1 Completed Appendix 1 Interconnection Request Form

The form requires the following information:

**Project Information:**
- Proposed Project Name
- Type of request (new facility, capacity increase/modification, commencement of market participation, or change from NR to CNR)
- Type of Interconnection Service requested: **Network Resource Interconnection Service (NR)** — energy capability only, OR **Capacity Network Resource Interconnection Service (CNR)** — energy and capacity capability
- Address/Location of Facility (Town/City, County, State)
- Requested Point of Interconnection (definitive POI required)
- Type of Generating Facility to be constructed
- Electric storage details (if applicable): charging capability, reactive capability, frequency response operating range
- Generating Facility Fuel Type
- Generating Facility Capacity (MW) at multiple temperature thresholds:
  - At or above 90°F, 50°F, 20°F, and 0°F
  - Maximum Gross MW, Maximum Net MW, and Net MW at POI for each temperature
- Requested Interconnection Service levels in MW (CNR and NR Capability for Summer and Winter)
- General description of equipment configuration and control technologies
- Requested Commercial Operation Date, Initial Synchronization Date, and In-Service Date

**Source**: [Appendix 1 Interconnection Request Form](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100030/oatt-schedule-22-appendix-1-large-generator-lgip-interconnection-request-with-attachments.pdf)

### 4.2 Site Control Demonstration

| Interconnection Service | Site Control Requirement |
|---|---|
| **CNR Interconnection Service** | **100% exclusive Site Control** in Interconnection Customer's name — no exceptions |
| **NR Interconnection Service** | 100% exclusive Site Control, **OR** (if Regulatory Limitations exist): (a) $10,000/MW deposit (min $500,000, max $2,000,000), (b) signed officer affidavit, and (c) documentation of Regulatory Limitations |
| **Existing Facility Modification** | Certification that Site Control exists and no additional real property is required |

**What constitutes Site Control** (per the [June 2025 Technical Notice](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100024/notice_re_regulatory_limitations_and_site_control_6_2_25.pdf)):
- Fee simple ownership or easement
- Valid written leasehold or contractual interest
- Valid written option to purchase or leasehold
- Executed written contract to purchase/acquire interest
- Filed applications for required permits on federal or state property

**Regulatory Limitation** must be a federal, state, local, or Tribal law making it infeasible to obtain Site Control prior to submission — permitting or siting issues alone do not qualify. Full Site Control must be demonstrated within **180 Calendar Days** of the effective date of executing an Interconnection Agreement.

### 4.3 Technical Data and Models

All data and models included in Appendix 1 and its associated attachments must be provided with the Interconnection Request during the Cluster Request Window. ISO-NE recommends submitting at least **15 business days** prior to close of the CRW to allow time for review and cure of deficiencies.

**Source**: [ISO-NE January 2024 TC Presentation (Q3)](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100007/a03.1b_2024_01_23_tc_order2023_compliance_presentation.pdf)

### 4.4 Customer Information

- Company Name (must match ISO Customer ID registration)
- ISO Customer ID#
- Company Address
- Company Representative (Name, Title, Contact Information)
- Authorized Signature

### 4.5 All Required Deposits

The IR must be accompanied by:
- $50,000 Initial Deposit
- $250,000 Study Deposit (or $100,000 for NR-to-CNR change requests)
- Commercial Readiness Deposit per Order No. 2023
- Site Control documentation or Site Control Deposit (if applicable)

Commercial Readiness Deposits may be delivered by electronic transfer to the System Operator's bank account, through an irrevocable Letter of Credit from an approved financial institution, a Surety Bond, or a combination thereof.

**Source**: [LGIP Section 3.1 per Feb 2024 TC Presentation](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100008/a03b_2024_02_15_tc_order2023_redlines_update_presentation.pdf)

---

## 5. Cluster Study Process Timeline

After submission of a valid IR during the Cluster Request Window:

| Phase | Duration | Key Activities |
|---|---|---|
| **Cluster Request Window** | 45 Calendar Days | Submit IR with all deposits, site control, and technical data |
| **Customer Engagement Window** | 60 Calendar Days | Cluster Study Scoping Meeting with all ICs; execute Cluster Study Agreement |
| **Cluster Study** | 270 Calendar Days* | Power flow, short circuit, stability, and EMT analysis; non-binding cost estimates for network upgrades |
| **Cluster Restudy** (if required) | 150 Calendar Days | Additional deposit required; updated network upgrade cost assignment |

*ISO-NE received an independent entity variation from Order No. 2023's 150-day requirement to allow 270 days, consistent with the established timeline for System Impact Studies in New England.

**Source**: [ISO-NE September 2023 TC Presentation](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100003/a04a_2023_09_27_tc_order2023_compliance_overview.pdf), [Interconnection Process Guide](https://www.iso-ne.com/participate/applications-status-changes/interconnection-process-guide)

---

## 6. Summary of Total Upfront Financial Commitment

For a new large generator requesting CNR Interconnection Service with full Site Control:

| Deposit Type | Amount |
|---|---|
| Initial Deposit (potentially non-refundable) | $50,000 |
| Study Deposit (refundable) | $250,000 |
| Commercial Readiness Deposit | Per Order No. 2023 |
| **Minimum Upfront Cash/LOC** | **$300,000 + CRD** |

If Site Control cannot be demonstrated due to Regulatory Limitations (NR IS only), add **$10,000/MW** (min $500,000, max $2,000,000).

---

## 7. Key Governing Documents

All requirements above are governed by the following documents, with the ISO Tariff taking precedence in case of any discrepancy:

1. **Schedule 22 – Large Generator Interconnection Procedures (LGIP)** (effective 5/3/25, ER25-2149-000): [PDF](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/regulatory/tariff/sect_2/sch22/sch_22_lgip.pdf)
2. **Appendix 1 – Interconnection Request Form**: [PDF](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100030/oatt-schedule-22-appendix-1-large-generator-lgip-interconnection-request-with-attachments.pdf)
3. **ISO-NE Interconnection Process Guide**: [Webpage](https://www.iso-ne.com/participate/applications-status-changes/interconnection-process-guide)
4. **ISO-NE Planning Procedure No. 5-6** (effective 10/11/25): [PDF](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/rules_proceds/isone_plan/pp05_6/pp5_6.pdf)
5. **Operating Procedure No. 14** – Technical Requirements for Generators
6. **Technical Notice on Site Control and Regulatory Limitations** (6/2/25): [PDF](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100024/notice_re_regulatory_limitations_and_site_control_6_2_25.pdf)
7. **FERC Order No. 2023 Compliance

      "text": "Submit an Interconnection Request During Cluster Request Window\n\n/isoexpress/webservices/-/webservice/getSignInInfo\n\n/isoexpress/webservices/-/webservice/authenticate\n\n# Submit an Interconnection Request During Cluster Request Window\n\n### In this section\n\n- Interconnection Process Guide\n- Is the ISO New England Interconnection Process Right for You?\n- Register for an IRTT Account\n- Submit an Interconnection Request During Cluster Request Window\n- Register Your Asset\n\n### Related Links\n\n- Glossary of Interconnection-Related Acronyms\n- Interconnection-Related Governing Documents\n- Contact IRTT Support\n\nOnce you’ve confirmed that your project is eligible for ISO New England’s interconnection process and you’ve received a customer ID, obtained Interconnection Request (IR) critical energy infrastructure information (CEII) clearance, and created an account in the Interconnection Request Tracking System (IRTT), you’re ready to submit an interconnection request (IR) during the 45 calendar day Cluster Request Window.\n\nOn this page:\n\n- Do Sufficient Research\n- Select Type of Interconnection Service\n- Submit Your Interconnection Request\n\n- Register for Market Participation\n- Questions?\n\n## Prepare for Next Cluster Request Window\n\nTo ensure success during the Interconnection Process, please review the information below:\n\n- Check the ISO Interconnection Request Queue for other projects already proposed in the area where you’re planning to interconnect. Planning Procedure No. 5-6 (PP 5-6), Interconnection Planning Procedure for Generation and Elective Transmission Upgrades, describes the scope and assumptions of interconnection studies.       "text": "Interconnection Process Guide\n\n/isoexpress/webservices/-/webservice/getSignInInfo\n\n/isoexpress/webservices/-/webservice/authenticate\n\n# Interconnection Process Guide\n\n### In this section\n\n- Interconnection Process Guide\n- Is the ISO New England Interconnection Process Right for You?\n- Register for an IRTT Account\n- Submit an Interconnection Request During Cluster Request Window\n- Register Your Asset\n\n### Related Links\n\n- Glossary of Interconnection-Related Acronyms\n- Interconnection-Related Governing Documents\n- Contact IRTT Support\n\nISO New England is providing this web guide to assist you in understanding the interconnection process for small and large generating facilities and elective transmission upgrades (ETUs) to interconnect to the administered transmission system. However, you shouldn’t rely solely on this web guide. Consult the ISO New England Inc. Transmission, Markets, and Services Tariff(ISO Tariff), Section II, known as the ISO’s Open Access Transmission Tariff(OATT), and relevant planning procedures and operating procedures to ensure that you are complying with all requirements. In case of any discrepancy with this web guide, the ISO Tariff and applicable operating procedures and planning procedures govern.\n\n## Overview of the Interconnection Process\n\nAs a Regional Transmission Organization (RTO), the ISO coordinates how transmission lines, power plants, and other resources connect to and operate on New England’s administered transmission system to ensure reliability and facilitate wholesale electricity market participation. The ISO does not administer the interconnection process for projects subject to state interconnection processes. Thus, after you determine that the ISO interconnection process applies to your project, you’ll need to submit an interconnection request to the ISO through the Interconnection Request Tracking Tool(IRTT). Existing customers can register for an account using IRTT, while new customers must first or simultaneously obtain an ISO customer ID and access to critical energy infrastructure information.\n\nAs required by the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission’s Order No. 2023, it is only possible to submit interconnection requests during a cluster request window. The next window to submit a complete interconnection request package for the next Cluster Study is expected to be open from October 5, 2026 to November 19, 2026. After the ISO validates your interconnection request and the cluster request window closes, the customer engagement window opens and a cluster study scoping meeting is held. A cluster study scoping meeting will address pertinent information about your project and the interconnection studies the ISO will perform to assess your project’s impact on the power system and the facilities and upgrades potentially needed to interconnect your project per all requirements and standards.\n\nAfter the ISO has completed the necessary study work, you’ll work with the ISO and the transmission owner on an Interconnection Agreement (IA), a formal document detailing the terms and conditions for interconnection service. You must enter into an IA before you can construct or operate a generator or an elective transmission upgrade. Final steps of the interconnection process are to submit final data and register your asset with the ISO.\n\n## Governing Documents\n\nSeveral schedules of the ISO’s Open Access Transmission Tariff contain the rules detailing the interconnection process:\n\nSection II, ISO New England Open Access Transmission Tariff(OATT)\n\n- Helps ensure that all transmission owners and transmission customers have open access to transmission service in New England\n- Details the rights and responsibilities of transmission owners and transmission customers as well as the procedures they must follow and the fees transmission customers must pay to access the transmission system\n- Sets out the transmission system planning process for the region\n\n## Resources and Tools\n\n- ISO Planning Procedure No. 5-6(PP 5-6), Scope of Study for System Impact Studies under the Generation Interconnection Procedures—describes the scope of interconnection studies conducted pursuant to Schedule 22, Schedule 23, and Schedule 25.\n- For additional resources for the Interconnection Process see Interconnection Service.\n- “Interconnection Process” training is available on the Training Materials webpage.\n- See the IRTT public queue. (Log in not necessary.) For information on the public version of the ISO New England Interconnection (ISO Queue), visit the Interconnection Request Queue webpage.\n- See the ISO Interconnection Request Tracking Tool (IRTT) and IRTT User Guide.\n- See the Interconnection Request Studies page to view completed studies for projects listed in the ISO’s Interconnection Request Queue (IR CEII required to view).\n- Interconnection frequently asked questions can be found in Ask ISO.\n- See Order No. 2023 Key Project page to access documents and resources related to the Order No. 2023: Improvements to Generator Interconnection Procedures and Agreements.\n- Read ISO-NE’s September 30, 2025, responses to Order Nos. 2023/2023-A transition-related questions\n- Read the Interconnection Request Technical Data Submittal Guidance document for guidance on submitting the technical data required for a Cluster Study.\n\n## Questions\n\nEmail IRTT Participant Support at irtt@iso-ne.com, or contact ISO Participant Support.\n\n## Interconnection Documents\n\n### Filters\n\nFilter this list ↑\n\n### Documents\n\n| A listing of links to documents. | Title and Description | Date | Type | Size |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\n\nSorry, no results are available with this choice of filter(s).       "text": "Transmission, Markets, and Services Tariff\n\n/isoexpress/webservices/-/webservice/getSignInInfo\n\n/isoexpress/webservices/-/webservice/authenticate\n\n# Transmission, Markets, and Services Tariff\n\n### In this section\n\n### Related Links\n\n- Regulatory Filings and Orders\n- Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC)\n- New England States Committee on Electricity (NESCOE)\n\nCreated and authorized by the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC), ISO New England operates under the ISO New England Inc. Known as the ISO Tariff, this document details: the rates, terms, and conditions for transmission, market, and other services provided by ISO New England; the rights and responsibilities of the ISO and market participants; and various schedules that define the revenues the ISO collects for its operations and those of the New England States Committee on Electricity (NESCOE).\n\nThe ISO Tariff, in conjunction with the ISO’s Transmission Operating Agreements, also helps satisfy FERC requirements for the ISO’s functioning as a Regional Transmission Organization (RTO).\n\n#### Section I–General Terms and Conditions\n\nThis section includes:\n\n- General Terms and Conditions(10/07/2025)—defined terms and certain rights, privileges, and obligations of all parties, and actions they may or must take under the ISO Tariff\n- Exhibit IA—The Financial Assurance Policy(09/01/2025)\n- Exhibit IB(08/10/2017)—reserved for future use\n- Exhibit IC(08/10/2017)—reserved for future use\n- Exhibit ID—The Billing Policy(02/20/2023)\n\n#### Section II—Open Access Transmission Tariff (OATT)\n\nVisit the OATT page. The OATT:\n\n- Helps ensure that all transmission owners and transmission customers have fair and open access to transmission service in New England\n- Details the rights and responsibilities of transmission owners and transmission customers, as well as the procedures they must follow and the fees transmission customers must pay to access the transmission system\n- Sets out the transmission system planning process for the region\n- Sets out the cost recovery and allocation mechanisms for transmission and ancillary services in the region\n\n#### Section III—Market Rule 1\n\nVisit the Market Rule 1 page. Market Rule 1 governs the operation of New England’s wholesale electricity markets. It includes detailed information on pricing, scheduling, offering, bidding, settlement, and other procedures related to the purchase and sale of electricity.\n\n#### Section IV—ISO Funding Mechanisms\n\nThis section details how ISO New England collects funds to pay for its functioning.\n\n- Section IV.A—Recovery of ISO Administrative Expenses(01/01/2026) is known as the Self-Funding Tariff. The collection of monies for NESCOE operation is also detailed, because the ISO acts as its billing and collection agent.\n- Section IV.B—Capital Funding Arrangements Tariff(01/01/2014) describes how the ISO funds capital projects.\n\n### Attachments to the ISO Tariff\n\n#### Attachment A—Market Participant Service Agreement: (06/17/2013)\n\nThis agreement provides the rights and responsibilities of businesses or organizations that generate, buy, sell, and transport electricity in the New England markets.\n\n#### Attachment A-1: (06/15/2013)\n\nThis attachment is reserved for future filings.\n\n#### Attachment B—Pro Forma ITC Operating Agreement: (08/30/2010)\n\nThis attachment is reserved for future filings.\n\n#### Attachment D—ISO New England Information Policy: (07/26/2024)\n\nThis policy provides the rules and procedures the ISO and stakeholders follow in handling the information collected and created in the administration of New England’s wholesale electricity markets and the operation of the region’s transmission grid.       "text": "FEBRUARY 15, 2024 | NEPOOL TRANSMISSION COMMITTEE | DOUBLETREE HOTEL, MILFORD, M A\n\nAl McBride D I R E C T O R | T R A N S M I S S I O N S E R V I C E S & R E S O U R C E Q U A L I F I C A T I O N\n\n#### Summary of Tariff Redline Updates since the January TC Meeting\n\n## Order No. 2023 – Improvements to Generator Interconnection Procedures and Agreements\n\nISO-NE PUBLIC 2\n\n### Presentation Overview\n\n• ISO has posted an updated draft of all Tariff redlines associated with Order No. 2023 compliance \n• This presentation summarizes the major updates to the Tariff redlines since the January TC meeting \n• Focus on Large Generator Interconnection Procedures (LGIP) in Schedule 22 of the Open Access Transmission Tariff (OATT) – The Small Generator Interconnection Procedures (SGIP) in Schedule 23 of the OATT have been overhauled to mirror the LGIP – The Elective Transmission Upgrade Interconnection Procedures (ETUIP) in Schedule 25 of the OATT also closely match the LGIP \n\nISO-NE PUBLIC ISO-NE INTERNAL USE\n\n### UPDATES TO THE TARIFF REDLINES SINCE THE\n\n### JANUARY TC MEETING\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Section 3.1\n\nCommercial Readiness Deposits that must be submitted to the System Operator under this LGIP must be (a) delivered to the System Operator’s bank account by electronic transfer, or (b) through the provision and maintenance of an irrevocable lLetter of cCredit in a form and from a financial institution acceptable to System Operator and included on the List of Eligible Letter of Credit Issuers, as described on the System Operator’s public website, or (c) a combination thereof. Each letter of credit must specify the Interconnection Request to which it corresponds. All costs associated with obtaining a letter of credit shall be borne by the Interconnection Customer.       "text": "Submit an Interconnection Request During Cluster Request Window\n\n/isoexpress/webservices/-/webservice/getSignInInfo\n\n/isoexpress/webservices/-/webservice/authenticate\n\n# Submit an Interconnection Request During Cluster Request Window\n\n### In this section\n\n- Interconnection Process Guide\n- Is the ISO New England Interconnection Process Right for You?\n- Register for an IRTT Account\n- Submit an Interconnection Request During Cluster Request Window\n- Register Your Asset\n\n### Related Links\n\n- Glossary of Interconnection-Related Acronyms\n- Interconnection-Related Governing Documents\n- Contact IRTT Support\n\nOnce you’ve confirmed that your project is eligible for ISO New England’s interconnection process and you’ve received a customer ID, obtained Interconnection Request (IR) critical energy infrastructure information (CEII) clearance, and created an account in the Interconnection Request Tracking System (IRTT), you’re ready to submit an interconnection request (IR) during the 45 calendar day Cluster Request Window.\n\nOn this page:\n\n- Do Sufficient Research\n- Select Type of Interconnection Service\n- Submit Your Interconnection Request\n\n- Register for Market Participation\n- Questions?\n\n## Prepare for Next Cluster Request Window\n\nTo ensure success during the Interconnection Process, please review the information below:\n\n- Check the ISO Interconnection Request Queue for other projects already proposed in the area where you’re planning to interconnect. You may find completed analyses for these projects useful—see the Interconnection Request Studies page (IR CEII required).\n- Study the interconnection materials that apply to your project—look for the applicable ISO Tariff schedule in the table below.\n- Carefully review all technical requirements for each study before submitting an interconnection request. Planning Procedure No. 5-6 (PP 5-6), Interconnection Planning Procedure for Generation and Elective Transmission Upgrades, describes the scope and assumptions of interconnection studies. Operating Procedure 14 (OP-14), Technical Requirements For Generators, Demand-Response Resources, Asset-Related Demands, and Alternative Technology Regulation Resources, identifies requirements that help ensure the reliable and efficient operation of market resources.\n- Use public information, such as FERC Form No. 715, Annual Transmission Planning and Evaluation Reports(IR CEII required), to do your own preliminary analysis of the system. Look for special considerations, such as weak grid issues, that could impact your project.\n\n## Decide on Type of Interconnection Service\n\nYour IR must indicate the type of interconnection service your project requests:\n\n- Network resource (NR) or network import (NI) interconnection service (energy capability only)—This enables participation in all energy markets, except the capacity market. If later you wish to participate in the capacity market, you’ll have to submit a new interconnection request for capacity network interconnection service.\n- Capacity network resource (CNR) or capacity network import (CNI) interconnection service (energy capability and capacity capability)—This enables participation in the energy and capacity markets.       "text": "Interconnection Process Guide\n\n/isoexpress/webservices/-/webservice/getSignInInfo\n\n/isoexpress/webservices/-/webservice/authenticate\n\n# Interconnection Process Guide\n\n### In this section\n\n- Interconnection Process Guide\n- Is the ISO New England Interconnection Process Right for You?\n- Register for an IRTT Account\n- Submit an Interconnection Request During Cluster Request Window\n- Register Your Asset\n\n### Related Links\n\n- Glossary of Interconnection-Related Acronyms\n- Interconnection-Related Governing Documents\n- Contact IRTT Support\n\nISO New England is providing this web guide to assist you in understanding the interconnection process for small and large generating facilities and elective transmission upgrades (ETUs) to interconnect to the administered transmission system. However, you shouldn’t rely solely on this web guide. Consult the ISO New England Inc. Transmission, Markets, and Services Tariff(ISO Tariff), Section II, known as the ISO’s Open Access Transmission Tariff(OATT), and relevant planning procedures and operating procedures to ensure that you are complying with all requirements. In case of any discrepancy with this web guide, the ISO Tariff and applicable operating procedures and planning procedures govern.\n\n## Overview of the Interconnection Process\n\nAs a Regional Transmission Organization (RTO), the ISO coordinates how transmission lines, power plants, and other resources connect to and operate on New England’s administered transmission system to ensure reliability and facilitate wholesale electricity market participation. The ISO does not administer the interconnection process for projects subject to state interconnection processes. Thus, after you determine that the ISO interconnection process applies to your project, you’ll need to submit an interconnection request to the ISO through the Interconnection Request Tracking Tool(IRTT). Existing customers can register for an account using IRTT, while new customers must first or simultaneously obtain an ISO customer ID and access to critical energy infrastructure information.\n\nAs required by the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission’s Order No. 2023, it is only possible to submit interconnection requests during a cluster request window. The next window to submit a complete interconnection request package for the next Cluster Study is expected to be open from October 5, 2026 to November 19, 2026. After the ISO validates your interconnection request and the cluster request window closes, the customer engagement window opens and a cluster study scoping meeting is held. A cluster study scoping meeting will address pertinent information about your project and the interconnection studies the ISO will perform to assess your project’s impact on the power system and the facilities and upgrades potentially needed to interconnect your project per all requirements and standards.\n\nAfter the ISO has completed the necessary study work, you’ll work with the ISO and the transmission owner on an Interconnection Agreement (IA), a formal document detailing the terms and conditions for interconnection service. You must enter into an IA before you can construct or operate a generator or an elective transmission upgrade. Final steps of the interconnection process are to submit final data and register your asset with the ISO.\n\n## Governing Documents\n\nSeveral schedules of the ISO’s Open Access Transmission Tariff contain the rules detailing the interconnection process:\n\nSection II, ISO New England Open Access Transmission Tariff(OATT)\n\n- Helps ensure that all transmission owners and transmission customers have open access to transmission service in New England\n- Details the rights and responsibilities of transmission owners and transmission customers as well as the procedures they must follow and the fees transmission customers must pay to access the transmission system\n- Sets out the transmission system planning process for the region\n\n## Resources and Tools\n\n- ISO Planning Procedure No. 5-6(PP 5-6), Scope of Study for System Impact Studies under the Generation Interconnection Procedures—describes the scope of interconnection studies conducted pursuant to Schedule 22, Schedule 23, and Schedule 25.\n- For additional resources for the Interconnection Process see Interconnection Service.\n- “Interconnection Process” training is available on the Training Materials webpage.\n- See the IRTT public queue. (Log in not necessary.) For information on the public version of the ISO New England Interconnection (ISO Queue), visit the Interconnection Request Queue webpage.\n- See the ISO Interconnection Request Tracking Tool (IRTT) and IRTT User Guide.\n- See the Interconnection Request Studies page to view completed studies for projects listed in the ISO’s Interconnection Request Queue (IR CEII required to view).\n- Interconnection frequently asked questions can be found in Ask ISO.\n- See Order No. 2023 Key Project page to access documents and resources related to the Order No. 2023: Improvements to Generator Interconnection Procedures and Agreements.\n- Read ISO-NE’s September 30, 2025, responses to Order Nos. 2023/2023-A transition-related questions\n- Read the Interconnection Request Technical Data Submittal Guidance document for guidance on submitting the technical data required for a Cluster Study.\n\n## Questions\n\nEmail IRTT Participant Support at irtt@iso-ne.com, or contact ISO Participant Support.\n\n## Interconnection Documents\n\n### Filters\n\nFilter this list ↑\n\n### Documents\n\n| A listing of links to documents. | Title and Description | Date | Type | Size |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\n\nSorry, no results are available with this choice of filter(s).       "text": "Transmission, Markets, and Services Tariff\n\n/isoexpress/webservices/-/webservice/getSignInInfo\n\n/isoexpress/webservices/-/webservice/authenticate\n\n# Transmission, Markets, and Services Tariff\n\n### In this section\n\n### Related Links\n\n- Regulatory Filings and Orders\n- Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC)\n- New England States Committee on Electricity (NESCOE)\n\nCreated and authorized by the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC), ISO New England operates under the ISO New England Inc. Transmission, Markets, and Services Tariff. Known as the ISO Tariff, this document details: the rates, terms, and conditions for transmission, market, and other services provided by ISO New England; the rights and responsibilities of the ISO and market participants; and various schedules that define the revenues the ISO collects for its operations and those of the New England States Committee on Electricity (NESCOE).\n\nThe ISO Tariff, in conjunction with the ISO’s Transmission Operating Agreements, also helps satisfy FERC requirements for the ISO’s functioning as a Regional Transmission Organization (RTO).\n\n#### Section I–General Terms and Conditions\n\nThis section includes:\n\n- General Terms and Conditions(10/07/2025)—defined terms and certain rights, privileges, and obligations of all parties, and actions they may or must take under the ISO Tariff\n- Exhibit IA—The Financial Assurance Policy(09/01/2025)\n- Exhibit IB(08/10/2017)—reserved for future use\n- Exhibit IC(08/10/2017)—reserved for future use\n- Exhibit ID—The Billing Policy(02/20/2023)\n\n#### Section II—Open Access Transmission Tariff (OATT)\n\nVisit the OATT page. The OATT:\n\n- Helps ensure that all transmission owners and transmission customers have fair and open access to transmission service in New England\n- Details the rights and responsibilities of transmission owners and transmission customers, as well as the procedures they must follow and the fees transmission customers must pay to access the transmission system\n- Sets out the transmission system planning process for the region\n- Sets out the cost recovery and allocation mechanisms for transmission and ancillary services in the region\n\n#### Section III—Market Rule 1\n\nVisit the Market Rule 1 page. Market Rule 1 governs the operation of New England’s wholesale electricity markets. It includes detailed information on pricing, scheduling, offering, bidding, settlement, and other procedures related to the purchase and sale of electricity.\n\n#### Section IV—ISO Funding Mechanisms\n\nThis section details how ISO New England collects funds to pay for its functioning.\n\n- Section IV.A—Recovery of ISO Administrative Expenses(01/01/2026) is known as the Self-Funding Tariff. It contains rates for the administrative functions of the ISO, including costs for scheduling and administering the movement of power through, out of, or within the balancing authority area; and costs for services the ISO provides to administer the energy and reliability markets. The collection of monies for NESCOE operation is also detailed, because the ISO acts as its billing and collection agent.\n- Section IV.B—Capital Funding Arrangements Tariff(01/01/2014) describes how the ISO funds capital projects.\n\n### Attachments to the ISO Tariff\n\n#### Attachment A—Market Participant Service Agreement: (06/17/2013)\n\nThis agreement provides the rights and responsibilities of businesses or organizations that generate, buy, sell, and transport electricity in the New England markets.\n\n#### Attachment A-1: (06/15/2013)\n\nThis attachment is reserved for future filings.\n\n#### Attachment B—Pro Forma ITC Operating Agreement: (08/30/2010)\n\nThis attachment is reserved for future filings.\n\n#### Attachment D—ISO New England Information Policy: (07/26/2024)\n\nThis policy provides the rules and procedures the ISO and stakeholders follow in handling the information collected and created in the administration of New England’s wholesale electricity markets and the operation of the region’s transmission grid. See the List of Authorized Persons and Authorized Commissions per the Information Policy.\n\n#### Attachment E—Service Agreements: (06/17/2013)\n\nThis attachment provides forms for service agreements under the ISO Tariff.\n\n#### Attachment F—Coordination Agreements: (01/01/2025)\n\nThese agreements document the ISO’s coordination of operations with operators of neighboring control areas and the terms for pricing emergency energy and security energy. Other available agreements document the relationship among asset owners of the facilities subject to such coordination agreements.\n\n- ISO-NE/NYISO List of Interconnection Facilities Joint ISO-NE/NYISO document listing the ISO-NE/NYISO Interconnections and description of each of the interties that makes up those interconnections (previously included as a part of the Transmission, Markets, and Services Tariff-Attachment F).\n\n#### Attachment G—Procedure to Protect for the Loss of Phase II Imports: (08/30/2010)\n\nThis procedure is designed to prevent the occurrence of a loss of Phase II contingency applicable when Phase II is operated in the isolated or synchronous mode.",

      "text": "## ISO-NE Responses to New Process Related Questions Raised in the Context of Order No. 2023 Compliance Discussions\n\nJanuary 18, 2024 General Notes Applicable to the ISO Responses Note there is overlap between many of the questions raised on the context of Order No. 2023 received by the ISO. Not all questions received by the ISO are documented below, but the questions and answers documented below attempt to provide responses to all received questions. The responses in this document reflect the ISO’s views of and expected compliance with Order No. 2023 at the time of its publication. These ISO views and proposals may change before the ISO presents its final Order No. 2023 compliance proposal. Participants should continually check for updates, follow the applicable stakeholder committee discussions and review the final version of the rules. Questions and Responses Q1: Will there be two different heat maps: one for NRIS and one for CNRIS? Will ISO-NE require that customers obtain CEII access to view the heat map(s)? Will Transmission Owners have a role in the creating and maintaining the heat map(s)? Response: Consistent with the requirements of Order No. 2023, which requires the heatmap to approximate capacity interconnection service and not energy interconnection service, ISO-NE will propose in the compliance filing that its heatmap will approximate CNR Interconnection Service. CEII access will not be required. ISO-NE is responsible for the creation and maintenance of the heat map. Transmission Owners will be responsible for providing ISO-NE with any supporting data, as needed. Q2: Will ISO-NE comply with Order 2023 by only allowing IR submittal during the Cluster Request Window? Response: Yes, ISO-NE will only allow Interconnection Request submittal during the Cluster Request Window, see proposed Section 3.4.2 of the LGIP. Q3: When is the technical data for the cluster study due? Will there be time for ISO-NE to review the data prior to the cluster study start?\n\nResponse: All data and models included in Appendix 1 of the IR and its associated attachments must be \nprovided with the Interconnection Request during the Cluster Request Window (CRW), see Section 3.4.2 \nof the LGIP. In order for ISO-NE to properly review and give the customer the full time to cure any \ndeficiencies, it is recommended to submit IR and data at least 15 business days prior to close of CRW. \nISO-NE will have five (5) Business Days to review the data and the Interconnection Customer will have \nup to ten (10) Business Days to cure any data deficiencies, but no later than the close of the Cluster \nRequest Window (Section 3.4.4 of LGIP). \nQ4: Will Cluster Request Windows open the same time every year, or will they depend on the stage that \nthe previous cluster cycle is in? Will Cluster Request Windows be delayed by additional restudies? Will \ncustomers have access to an updated heat map, cluster (re)study results, and base case from the most \nrecent cluster prior to entering the next cluster study? Will ISO-NE allow customers to update their \ntechnical data prior to the cluster study start if the cluster study start is delayed by a previous cluster \nneeding a restudy? \nResponse: The Initial Cluster Request Window will open sixty (60) Calendar Days after the conclusion of \nthe Transitional Study process. Subsequent Cluster Request Windows will open thirty (30) Calendar Days \nafter ISO-NE issues Facilities Study Agreements and/or draft Interconnection Agreements (if the \nFacilities Study is waived) to all Interconnection Customers in the previous Cluster Study. ISO-NE will \nprovide advance notice on its public website when each Cluster Request Window will open.       "text": "Interconnection Process Guide\n\n/isoexpress/webservices/-/webservice/getSignInInfo\n\n/isoexpress/webservices/-/webservice/authenticate\n\n# Interconnection Process Guide\n\n### In this section\n\n- Interconnection Process Guide\n- Is the ISO New England Interconnection Process Right for You?\n- Register for an IRTT Account\n- Submit an Interconnection Request During Cluster Request Window\n- Register Your Asset\n\n### Related Links\n\n- Glossary of Interconnection-Related Acronyms\n- Interconnection-Related Governing Documents\n- Contact IRTT Support\n\nISO New England is providing this web guide to assist you in understanding the interconnection process for small and large generating facilities and elective transmission upgrades (ETUs) to interconnect to the administered transmission system. However, you shouldn’t rely solely on this web guide. Consult the ISO New England Inc. Transmission, Markets, and Services Tariff(ISO Tariff), Section II, known as the ISO’s Open Access Transmission Tariff(OATT), and relevant planning procedures and operating procedures to ensure that you are complying with all requirements. In case of any discrepancy with this web guide, the ISO Tariff and applicable operating procedures and planning procedures govern.\n\n## Overview of the Interconnection Process\n\nAs a Regional Transmission Organization (RTO), the ISO coordinates how transmission lines, power plants, and other resources connect to and operate on New England’s administered transmission system to ensure reliability and facilitate wholesale electricity market participation. The ISO does not administer the interconnection process for projects subject to state interconnection processes. Thus, after you determine that the ISO interconnection process applies to your project, you’ll need to submit an interconnection request to the ISO through the Interconnection Request Tracking Tool(IRTT). Existing customers can register for an account using IRTT, while new customers must first or simultaneously obtain an ISO customer ID and access to critical energy infrastructure information.\n\nAs required by the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission’s Order No. 2023, it is only possible to submit interconnection requests during a cluster request window. The next window to submit a complete interconnection request package for the next Cluster Study is expected to be open from October 5, 2026 to November 19, 2026. After the ISO validates your interconnection request and the cluster request window closes, the customer engagement window opens and a cluster study scoping meeting is held. A cluster study scoping meeting will address pertinent information about your project and the interconnection studies the ISO will perform to assess your project’s impact on the power system and the facilities and upgrades potentially needed to interconnect your project per all requirements and standards.\n\nAfter the ISO has completed the necessary study work, you’ll work with the ISO and the transmission owner on an Interconnection Agreement (IA), a formal document detailing the terms and conditions for interconnection service. You must enter into an IA before you can construct or operate a generator or an elective transmission upgrade. Final steps of the interconnection process are to submit final data and register your asset with the ISO.\n\n## Governing Documents\n\nSeveral schedules of the ISO’s Open Access Transmission Tariff contain the rules detailing the interconnection process:\n\nSection II, ISO New England Open Access Transmission Tariff(OATT)\n\n- Helps ensure that all transmission owners and transmission customers have open access to transmission service in New England\n- Details the rights and responsibilities of transmission owners and transmission customers as well as the procedures they must follow and the fees transmission customers must pay to access the transmission system\n- Sets out the transmission system planning process for the region\n\n## Resources and Tools\n\n- ISO Planning Procedure No. 5-6(PP 5-6), Scope of Study for System Impact Studies under the Generation Interconnection Procedures—describes the scope of interconnection studies conducted pursuant to Schedule 22, Schedule 23, and Schedule 25.\n- For additional resources for the Interconnection Process see Interconnection Service.\n- “Interconnection Process” training is available on the Training Materials webpage.\n- See the IRTT public queue.       "text": "Interconnection Process Guide\n\n/isoexpress/webservices/-/webservice/getSignInInfo\n\n/isoexpress/webservices/-/webservice/authenticate\n\n# Interconnection Process Guide\n\n### In this section\n\n- Interconnection Process Guide\n- Is the ISO New England Interconnection Process Right for You?\n- Register for an IRTT Account\n- Submit an Interconnection Request During Cluster Request Window\n- Register Your Asset\n\n### Related Links\n\n- Glossary of Interconnection-Related Acronyms\n- Interconnection-Related Governing Documents\n- Contact IRTT Support\n\nISO New England is providing this web guide to assist you in understanding the interconnection process for small and large generating facilities and elective transmission upgrades (ETUs) to interconnect to the administered transmission system. However, you shouldn’t rely solely on this web guide. Consult the ISO New England Inc. Transmission, Markets, and Services Tariff(ISO Tariff), Section II, known as the ISO’s Open Access Transmission Tariff(OATT), and relevant planning procedures and operating procedures to ensure that you are complying with all requirements. In case of any discrepancy with this web guide, the ISO Tariff and applicable operating procedures and planning procedures govern.\n\n## Overview of the Interconnection Process\n\nAs a Regional Transmission Organization (RTO), the ISO coordinates how transmission lines, power plants, and other resources connect to and operate on New England’s administered transmission system to ensure reliability and facilitate wholesale electricity market participation. The ISO does not administer the interconnection process for projects subject to state interconnection processes. Thus, after you determine that the ISO interconnection process applies to your project, you’ll need to submit an interconnection request to the ISO through the Interconnection Request Tracking Tool(IRTT). Existing customers can register for an account using IRTT, while new customers must first or simultaneously obtain an ISO customer ID and access to critical energy infrastructure information.\n\nAs required by the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission’s Order No. 2023, it is only possible to submit interconnection requests during a cluster request window. The next window to submit a complete interconnection request package for the next Cluster Study is expected to be open from October 5, 2026 to November 19, 2026. After the ISO validates your interconnection request and the cluster request window closes, the customer engagement window opens and a cluster study scoping meeting is held. A cluster study scoping meeting will address pertinent information about your project and the interconnection studies the ISO will perform to assess your project’s impact on the power system and the facilities and upgrades potentially needed to interconnect your project per all requirements and standards.\n\nAfter the ISO has completed the necessary study work, you’ll work with the ISO and the transmission owner on an Interconnection Agreement (IA), a formal document detailing the terms and conditions for interconnection service. You must enter into an IA before you can construct or operate a generator or an elective transmission upgrade. Final steps of the interconnection process are to submit final data and register your asset with the ISO.\n\n## Governing Documents\n\nSeveral schedules of the ISO’s Open Access Transmission Tariff contain the rules detailing the interconnection process:\n\nSection II, ISO New England Open Access Transmission Tariff(OATT)\n\n- Helps ensure that all transmission owners and transmission customers have open access to transmission service in New England\n- Details the rights and responsibilities of transmission owners and transmission customers as well as the procedures they must follow and the fees transmission customers must pay to access the transmission system\n- Sets out the transmission system planning process for the region\n\n## Resources and Tools\n\n- ISO Planning Procedure No. 5-6(PP 5-6), Scope of Study for System Impact Studies under the Generation Interconnection Procedures—describes the scope of interconnection studies conducted pursuant to Schedule 22, Schedule 23, and Schedule 25.\n- For additional resources for the Interconnection Process see Interconnection Service.\n- “Interconnection Process” training is available on the Training Materials webpage.\n- See the IRTT public queue. # Requirements and Fees for Submitting a Valid Large Generator Interconnection Application in ISO New England

Based on the latest ISO-NE Interconnection Process Guide, Schedule 22 LGIP (effective 5/3/25, ER25-2149-000), and supporting Order No. 2023 compliance documents, below is a comprehensive summary of the requirements and fees.

---

## 1. Prerequisites Before Submission

| Requirement | Details | Source |
|---|---|---|
| **ISO Customer ID** | Must obtain an ISO Customer ID before or simultaneously with submission | [Interconnection Process Guide](https://www.iso-ne.com/participate/applications-status-changes/interconnection-process-guide) |
| **IR CEII Clearance** | Must obtain Interconnection Request Critical Energy Infrastructure Information access (can take up to 2 weeks) | [Tips for submitting TCS Agreement](https://askiso.iso-ne.com/s/article/Tips-for-submitting-a-Transitional-Cluster-Study-Agreement-package) |
| **IRTT Account** | Must create an account in the Interconnection Request Tracking Tool (IRTT) and be assigned the appropriate role | [Interconnection Process Guide](https://www.iso-ne.com/participate/applications-status-changes/interconnection-process-guide) |

---

## 2. Submission Window

Interconnection Requests can **only** be submitted during a **45 calendar day Cluster Request Window**, as required by FERC Order No. 2023.

- **Next Cluster Request Window**: Expected **October 5, 2026 to November 19, 2026**
- ISO-NE will have **5 Business Days** to review the data after receipt
- Interconnection Customer will have up to **10 Business Days** to cure any data deficiencies, but no later than the close of the Cluster Request Window

**Source**: [Interconnection Process Guide](https://www.iso-ne.com/participate/applications-status-changes/interconnection-process-guide) and [ISO-NE January 2024 TC Presentation (LGIP Section 3.4.4)](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100007/a03.1b_2024_01_23_tc_order2023_compliance_presentation.pdf)

---

## 3. Required Fees and Deposits

Per LGIP Section 3.4.1.1 and the Order No. 2023 compliance filings, the following financial commitments are required at the time of Interconnection Request submission:

| Fee/Deposit | Amount | Refundability | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Initial Deposit** | **$50,000** | Potentially non-refundable | Applied toward ISO costs for processing the IR, developing study agreements and attachments, and developing the LGIA |
| **Study Deposit (Cluster Study)** | **$250,000** (new NR or CNR requests) or **$100,000** (requests to change from existing NR to CNR) | Refundable (applied to Cluster Study costs) | Covers the Interconnection Customer's share of Cluster Study costs |
| **Commercial Readiness Deposit (CRD)** | Per Order No. 2023 requirements (ISO-NE adopted the federal standard) | Refundable minus applicable withdrawal penalties | Demonstrates commercial readiness; can be provided as cash, irrevocable Letter of Credit, Surety Bond, or combination |
| **Site Control Deposit** (alternative for NR IS only) | **$10,000/MW**, subject to a minimum of **$500,000** and maximum of **$2,000,000** | Refundable within the cure period (per Section 3.4.3) | Only available when Regulatory Limitations prevent demonstration of 100% Site Control for NR Interconnection Service |

**Source**: [LGIP Section 3.4.1.1 via Jan 2024 TC Presentation](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100007/a03.1b_2024_01_23_tc_order2023_compliance_presentation.pdf), [ISO-NE Transition Q&A (Q16, Q17)](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100006/2023_12_15_order_no_2023_transition_question_and_answer.pdf), [Appendix 1 IR Form](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100030/oatt-schedule-22-appendix-1-large-generator-lgip-interconnection-request-with-attachments.pdf)

### Additional Downstream Costs (Post-Submission)

| Item | Details |
|---|---|
| **Cluster Restudy Deposit** | Additional deposit for 5% of network upgrade cost assignment from the Cluster Study |
| **Interconnection Facilities Study Deposit** | Determined by the Interconnecting Transmission Owner and ISO |
| **Affected System Study Costs** | Interconnection Customer responsible for costs of studies of Internal Affected Systems (LGIP Section 3.4.4.1) |

---

## 4. Valid Interconnection Request Requirements

Per LGIP Section 3.4 and Appendix 1, a valid Interconnection Request must include all of the following:

### 4.1 Completed Appendix 1 Interconnection Request Form

The form requires the following information:

**Project Information:**
- Proposed Project Name
- Type of request (new facility, capacity increase/modification, commencement of market participation, or change from NR to CNR)
- Type of Interconnection Service requested: **Network Resource Interconnection Service (NR)** — energy capability only, OR **Capacity Network Resource Interconnection Service (CNR)** — energy and capacity capability
- Address/Location of Facility (Town/City, County, State)
- Requested Point of Interconnection (definitive POI required)
- Type of Generating Facility to be constructed
- Electric storage details (if applicable): charging capability, reactive capability, frequency response operating range
- Generating Facility Fuel Type
- Generating Facility Capacity (MW) at multiple temperature thresholds:
  - At or above 90°F, 50°F, 20°F, and 0°F
  - Maximum Gross MW, Maximum Net MW, and Net MW at POI for each temperature
- Requested Interconnection Service levels in MW (CNR and NR Capability for Summer and Winter)
- General description of equipment configuration and control technologies
- Requested Commercial Operation Date, Initial Synchronization Date, and In-Service Date

**Source**: [Appendix 1 Interconnection Request Form](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100030/oatt-schedule-22-appendix-1-large-generator-lgip-interconnection-request-with-attachments.pdf)

### 4.2 Site Control Demonstration

| Interconnection Service | Site Control Requirement |
|---|---|
| **CNR Interconnection Service** | **100% exclusive Site Control** in Interconnection Customer's name — no exceptions |
| **NR Interconnection Service** | 100% exclusive Site Control, **OR** (if Regulatory Limitations exist): (a) $10,000/MW deposit (min $500,000, max $2,000,000), (b) signed officer affidavit, and (c) documentation of Regulatory Limitations |
| **Existing Facility Modification** | Certification that Site Control exists and no additional real property is required |

**What constitutes Site Control** (per the [June 2025 Technical Notice](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100024/notice_re_regulatory_limitations_and_site_control_6_2_25.pdf)):
- Fee simple ownership or easement
- Valid written leasehold or contractual interest
- Valid written option to purchase or leasehold
- Executed written contract to purchase/acquire interest
- Filed applications for required permits on federal or state property

**Regulatory Limitation** must be a federal, state, local, or Tribal law making it infeasible to obtain Site Control prior to submission — permitting or siting issues alone do not qualify. Full Site Control must be demonstrated within **180 Calendar Days** of the effective date of executing an Interconnection Agreement.

### 4.3 Technical Data and Models

All data and models included in Appendix 1 and its associated attachments must be provided with the Interconnection Request during the Cluster Request Window. ISO-NE recommends submitting at least **15 business days** prior to close of the CRW to allow time for review and cure of deficiencies.

**Source**: [ISO-NE January 2024 TC Presentation (Q3)](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100007/a03.1b_2024_01_23_tc_order2023_compliance_presentation.pdf)

### 4.4 Customer Information

- Company Name (must match ISO Customer ID registration)
- ISO Customer ID#
- Company Address
- Company Representative (Name, Title, Contact Information)
- Authorized Signature

### 4.5 All Required Deposits

The IR must be accompanied by:
- $50,000 Initial Deposit
- $250,000 Study Deposit (or $100,000 for NR-to-CNR change requests)
- Commercial Readiness Deposit per Order No. 2023
- Site Control documentation or Site Control Deposit (if applicable)

Commercial Readiness Deposits may be delivered by electronic transfer to the System Operator's bank account, through an irrevocable Letter of Credit from an approved financial institution, a Surety Bond, or a combination thereof.

**Source**: [LGIP Section 3.1 per Feb 2024 TC Presentation](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100008/a03b_2024_02_15_tc_order2023_redlines_update_presentation.pdf)

---

## 5. Cluster Study Process Timeline

After submission of a valid IR during the Cluster Request Window:

| Phase | Duration | Key Activities |
|---|---|---|
| **Cluster Request Window** | 45 Calendar Days | Submit IR with all deposits, site control, and technical data |
| **Customer Engagement Window** | 60 Calendar Days | Cluster Study Scoping Meeting with all ICs; execute Cluster Study Agreement |
| **Cluster Study** | 270 Calendar Days* | Power flow, short circuit, stability, and EMT analysis; non-binding cost estimates for network upgrades |
| **Cluster Restudy** (if required) | 150 Calendar Days | Additional deposit required; updated network upgrade cost assignment |

*ISO-NE received an independent entity variation from Order No. 2023's 150-day requirement to allow 270 days, consistent with the established timeline for System Impact Studies in New England.

**Source**: [ISO-NE September 2023 TC Presentation](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100003/a04a_2023_09_27_tc_order2023_compliance_overview.pdf), [Interconnection Process Guide](https://www.iso-ne.com/participate/applications-status-changes/interconnection-process-guide)

---

## 6. Summary of Total Upfront Financial Commitment

For a new large generator requesting CNR Interconnection Service with full Site Control:

| Deposit Type | Amount |
|---|---|
| Initial Deposit (potentially non-refundable) | $50,000 |
| Study Deposit (refundable) | $250,000 |
| Commercial Readiness Deposit | Per Order No. 2023 |
| **Minimum Upfront Cash/LOC** | **$300,000 + CRD** |

If Site Control cannot be demonstrated due to Regulatory Limitations (NR IS only), add **$10,000/MW** (min $500,000, max $2,000,000).

---

## 7. Key Governing Documents

All requirements above are governed by the following documents, with the ISO Tariff taking precedence in case of any discrepancy:

1. **Schedule 22 – Large Generator Interconnection Procedures (LGIP)** (effective 5/3/25, ER25-2149-000): [PDF](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/regulatory/tariff/sect_2/sch22/sch_22_lgip.pdf)
2. **Appendix 1 – Interconnection Request Form**: [PDF](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100030/oatt-schedule-22-appendix-1-large-generator-lgip-interconnection-request-with-attachments.pdf)
3. **ISO-NE Interconnection Process Guide**: [Webpage](https://www.iso-ne.com/participate/applications-status-changes/interconnection-process-guide)
4. **ISO-NE Planning Procedure No. 5-6** (effective 10/11/25): [PDF](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/rules_proceds/isone_plan/pp05_6/pp5_6.pdf)
5. **Operating Procedure No. 14** – Technical Requirements for Generators
6. **Technical Notice on Site Control and Regulatory Limitations** (6/2/25): [PDF](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100024/notice_re_regulatory_limitations_and_site_control_6_2_25.pdf)
7. **FERC Order No. 2023 Compliance
